## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- audit: Verify a spec unit's implementation against its authoritative spec. Reads the manifest, reads spec files, walks target code, catalogs every gap, and writes task files for each finding.
- spec: Read the implementation spec for a given I/O module. Usage: /spec <module>. Modules: console, process, designer, graphics, display-elements, shapes, opc
- design-qa: Inventory all implementations of a cross-cutting feature across the codebase, surface inconsistencies, ask targeted design questions, and write the answers as an authoritative decision file. Run this BEFORE /audit on any feature marked ⚠️ NOT SPECCED in the manifest.
- spec-index: Print a quick-reference index of every audit unit — unit ID, canonical name, wave, and current task status. Use this to look up the correct unit ID or module name before running spec-scout or feature-agent.
- agent-guide: Print a reference guide for all custom agents in this project — what each one does, when to use it, how to launch it, and usage examples.
- wrapup: Close the current work unit (summary + review + doc updates)
- review: Run a verification review of work done since the last [initprompt]
- deepreview: Run a deep verification review including full file contents
- summarize: Generate an LLM-based summary of the current work unit
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the diff carefully against what was asked in the prompt, looking for concerns.

The prompt asked to:
1. Fix token registry gaps in dependency order from ui-audit/06-claim-a-plan.md section 1 category (a)
2. Work only on tokens, not shell drift items
3. Don't touch canvas-layer files per Claim C deferral, except fixing token definitions in index.css
4. For each gap: state which gap, add token with value from audit evidence, verify no collision, search codebase for references
5. If gap turns out not to be a real gap, note it and skip
6. Update plan marking completed gaps with status flag and date


7. Don't modify 02-comparison.md or 04-recommendations.md

The diff covers index.css with token additions, PromoteToShapeWizard.tsx with one line changed, and the plan updated. But I'm seeing a problem—PromoteToShapeWizard.tsx shouldn't have been modified since the prompt explicitly says not to touch consumer files, only the token registry itself.

Looking closer though, the user did authorize this change in the second turn when they asked about stripping muted and adding subtle, and the assistant confirmed it. So this was scope expansion with explicit user approval, even though the initial prompt said hands off. The A8 plan entry still says "Action required in a consumer-file pass," which suggests this change might have been premature or should've been flagged differently.

Now I'm noticing the z-index values got reshuffled — dropdown went from 200 to 500, modal from 300 to 1000, toast from 700 to 2000, while emergency stayed at 800. That creates a problem where emergency at 800 is now lower than toast at 2000, which breaks the stacking order.

The plan flagged this as a concern but the tokens were committed anyway, even though the decision to use the full z-index scale was pre-decided in a previous session. The functional regression wasn't caught until the deep review happened.

I'm also seeing that `--io-font-sans` was only added to the `:root` selector but not to the individual theme blocks, which seems incomplete. I'm realizing the specificity issue here — `:root` and `[data-theme="light"]` have equal specificity, but since `[data-theme="light"]` appears later in the file, it wins through cascade order. However, they're actually targeting different elements: `:root` applies to the `html` element while `[data-theme="light"]` applies to the `body`, so the variables inherit down through the DOM tree.

When `[data-theme="light"]` sets variables on `body`, those override inherited values for descendants, but variables like `--io-font-sans` that aren't redefined there just fall back to the inherited `:root` value. Looking at the actual CSS structure, `--io-font-sans` is defined in the combined `:root, [data-theme="dark"]` block and isn't theme-specific since the font stack doesn't change between themes.

Now examining the z-index ordering, I notice `--io-z-command` is set to 400 while `--io-z-dropdown` jumped to 500, which means the command palette would render below dropdowns—potentially problematic since command palettes typically need to be the highest-priority overlay. The full stack shows dropdown at 500, modal at 1000, command at 400, visual-lock at 500, kiosk-auth at 600, toast at 2000, and emergency at 800, which suggests the ordering might need adjustment.

Looking at the actual values, this represents a regression from the previous state. Before this change, command was at 400 and dropdown was at 200, so command was correctly positioned above dropdowns. Now with dropdown bumped to 500, command has fallen below it, which inverts the intended hierarchy and seems like an unintended consequence of the dropdown adjustment.

The DoD item about defining two new tokens is also problematic — `--io-text-inverse` was already present in the codebase, so it shouldn't be listed as something that needed to be "defined." Only `--io-alarm-inactive` is actually new. The DoD was partially corrected in a second review (removing `--io-accent-muted` from item 1), but item 2 still incorrectly claims `--io-text-inverse` needed to be added when it was already there.

Looking at the plan note for the PromoteToShapeWizard.tsx change, it marked updating that file as "Action required," but the diff shows this was already completed. The plan's language about what still needs to be done is now outdated.

I'm also noticing the token count in the file header hasn't been updated — it still says 138 tokens, but the diff adds 10 new unique token names across the themes, so that number is now inaccurate. concerns:

**Primary concerns:**

1. **--io-z-command inverted relative to --io-z-dropdown** — Before the diff, command(400) > dropdown(200). After the diff, command(400) < dropdown(500). The command palette is now below dropdowns in the stacking order, which is a behavioral regression.

2. **PromoteToShapeWizard.tsx was modified despite the prompt prohibiting consumer file changes** — The prompt said "Do not modify any consumer files in this prompt." The change happened via user confirmation in a follow-up exchange, but the A8 plan entry still describes this as "Action required in a consumer-file pass," making the plan stale/incorrect about the current state.

3. **--io-z-emergency remains at 800, now below --io-z-toast at 2000** — The plan notes this as a concern, but the tokens were committed with this known functional inversion. Emergency overlays will now render below toast notifications in any code that uses these tokens.

4. **Token count comment not updated** — The header says "138 tokens" but ~10 new tokens were added.

Now I'm separating the real issues from noise. The z-command and z-dropdown inversion is legitimate—command was above dropdown before, now it's below. If any code actually uses both tokens, a command palette could get obscured by a dropdown. I should flag that the logs don't show a search for `--io-z-command` consumers, so the runtime impact might be zero if nothing's consuming it yet. The PromoteToShapeWizard change is worth noting since it contradicts the stated constraints, even though it was user-approved. , now lower) — so the command token dropped from 400 to 400 while dropdown jumped to 500, reversing their relationship. This is a new regression introduced by the diff, not a pre-existing issue. I should flag this as a silent z-index inversion that wasn't caught during review.

content.text:
## Summary

The diff correctly fills 11 of 14 token gaps (A1–A7, A9–A11, A13–A14), skips 2 with documented rationale (A8 consumer-fix preferred, A12 already defined), and updates the plan accurately. However, the z-index changes introduce two stacking-order inversions that weren't present before the diff, the PromoteToShapeWizard.tsx change was made despite the prompt explicitly prohibiting consumer file edits in this pass, and the A8 plan entry remains misleadingly stale about work that was already done.

## Concerns

**1. `--io-z-command` inverted relative to `--io-z-dropdown` — silent regression**

- **What:** Before the diff, `--io-z-command: 400` > `--io-z-dropdown: 200`. After the diff, `--io-z-command: 400` < `--io-z-dropdown: 500`. The command palette is now below dropdowns in the token scale.
- **Where:** `index.css` z-index block in all three theme blocks (dark ~line 209–220, light ~line 422, hphmi ~line 627).
- **Why this matters:** Any future code consuming these tokens for the command palette will render below an open dropdown. The pre-diff scale was consistent (command > dropdown); the new scale is not. The plan's collision note mentions `--io-z-visual-lock` and `--io-z-emergency` but does not mention this inversion.

**2. `--io-z-emergency: 800` is now below `--io-z-toast: 2000` — live stacking inversion**

- **What:** The plan flags this as a concern but commits the tokens anyway. Any code today that renders an emergency overlay alongside a toast will have the toast win. This isn't a future Claim B problem — it takes effect the moment any code uses both tokens.
- **Where:** All three theme z-index blocks; `--io-z-emergency` unchanged at 800, `--io-z-toast` raised from 700 to 2000.
- **Why this diverges from intent:** The plan's stated reasoning for Option B was to align with actual usage (1000–9999 range). Emergency signals by definition should outrank transient notifications. The diff creates a token scale that contradicts this hierarchy without resolving it first.

**3. PromoteToShapeWizard.tsx edited despite explicit prompt prohibition**

- **What:** The initial prompt says "Do not modify any consumer files in this prompt." The diff modifies `PromoteToShapeWizard.tsx:2165` (changing `var(--io-accent-muted, #3b82f6)` to `var(--io-accent-subtle)`).
- **Where:** `frontend/src/pages/designer/components/PromoteToShapeWizard.tsx`, line 2168 in pre-diff numbering.
- **Why this is a concern:** The change was made after a user follow-up exchange, which counts as authorization. But the A8 plan entry was not updated to reflect completion — it still says "**Action required in a consumer-file pass:** update `PromoteToShapeWizard.tsx:2168`..." This makes the plan's A8 status misleading: it's marked `⚠ Skipped — consumer fix preferred` but the consumer fix was already applied.

**4. Section 4 DoD item 2 is stale**

- **What:** DoD item 2 reads "Two new tokens defined. `--io-alarm-inactive` and `--io-text-inverse` exist in `index.css` with documented values." `--io-text-inverse` was pre-existing (A12 skip); the phrasing implies it was added in this workstream.
- **Where:** `ui-audit/06-claim-a-plan.md`, Section 4 item 2.
- **Why this matters:** Not a code bug, but the DoD criterion as written cannot be used to verify whether this workstream's work is complete — it conflates pre-existing state with new additions.

## Verification Notes

- All 11 completed tokens are correctly applied to all three theme blocks (dark/light/hphmi) — no theme was missed.
- `--io-font-sans` is correctly placed only in `:root` (consistent with `--io-font-mono` pattern); theme blocks inherit it.
- `--io-accent-rgb` per-theme values are arithmetically correct: dark `#2dd4bf` → `45 212 191`, light `#0d9488` → `13 148 136`, hphmi `#14b8a6` → `20 184 166`.
- The header comment "CSS Custom Properties (138 tokens)" was not updated after adding ~10 new token names; the count is now stale.
- The `--io-z-visual-lock: 500` collision with `--io-z-dropdown: 500` is correctly flagged in the plan.
